summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

A bag has 4 red and 6 blue marbles. Two marbles are drawn without replacement. What is P(both red)?

P(1st red) = 4/10. P(2nd red | 1st red) = 3/9. P(both red) = (4/10)(3/9) = 12/90 = 2/15.

Rolling a die twice: what is the probability of getting a 6 on both rolls?

Each roll is independent. P(6 and 6) = (1/6)(1/6) = 1/36.

What is the 'multiplication rule' for probability?

The general multiplication rule uses conditional probability. For independent events, P(B|A) = P(B), simplifying the formula.

What is the 'at least one' probability technique?

The complement method: P(at least one success) = 1 − P(zero successes). This is often easier to compute than adding all individual cases.

What is meant by 'testing for independence' using probability?

If P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B), the events satisfy the mathematical definition of independence.

What is the 'general multiplication rule'?

The general multiplication rule works for all event pairs. For independent events it simplifies since P(B|A) = P(B).

A drug test is 95% accurate. If two independent tests are administered to the same person, what is the probability both tests are correct?

P(both correct) = 0.95 × 0.95 = 0.9025. The tests are independent.

A jar contains 8 red and 12 blue candies. You eat one candy (without replacement), then pick another. What is the probability both are red?

P(1st red) = 8/20. P(2nd red|1st red) = 7/19. P(both red) = (8/20)(7/19) = 56/380 ≈ 0.147.

The probability of rain on Saturday is 0.3 and on Sunday is 0.5 (independently). What is the probability it rains on at least one day of the weekend?

P(no rain either day) = (1−0.3)(1−0.5) = 0.7 × 0.5 = 0.35. P(at least one day) = 1 − 0.35 = 0.65.

Three machines in a factory work independently. Machine A fails with probability 0.02, B with 0.03, and C with 0.05. What is the probability that all three machines work on a given day?

P(all work) = (1−0.02)(1−0.03)(1−0.05) = 0.98 × 0.97 × 0.95 ≈ 0.9030.

A deck of 52 cards is dealt face down. You draw 2 cards without replacement. What is the probability that both are aces?

P(1st ace) = 4/52. P(2nd ace|1st ace) = 3/51. P(both) = (4/52)(3/51) = 12/2652 = 1/221.

A security system has 3 independent alarm sensors. Each has a 90% chance of detecting an intruder. What is the probability that at least one sensor detects the intruder?

P(none detect) = 0.1 × 0.1 × 0.1 = 0.001. P(at least one) = 1 − 0.001 = 0.999.

A box has 3 defective items and 7 good items. Two items are drawn without replacement. What is the probability the first is defective AND the second is good?

P(1st defective) = 3/10. P(2nd good | 1st defective) = 7/9. P = (3/10)(7/9) = 21/90 = 7/30.

A basketball player makes free throws with probability 0.80, independently. What is the probability she makes 3 in a row?

P(3 makes) = 0.80 × 0.80 × 0.80 = 0.80³ = 0.512.

A medical lab runs two independent diagnostic tests. Test 1 has a 5% false positive rate and Test 2 has a 3% false positive rate. What is the probability a healthy person tests positive on BOTH tests?

P(both false positive) = 0.05 × 0.03 = 0.0015, or 0.15%. Using two independent tests dramatically reduces false positive risk.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: What is the 'multiplication rule' for probability?

The general multiplication rule uses conditional probability. For independent events, P(B|A) = P(B), simplifying the formula.

The general multiplication rule works for all event pairs. For independent events it simplifies since P(B|A) = P(B).
